meta.note: All figures provisional and subject to revision by the issuing statistical office.

markets.name: United Kingdom
markets.commentary: Headline held at 2.8%, core ticked up to 2.6% and services jumped to 3.7%. Input prices accelerated to 8.7% on crude oil inputs up 71.8% year on year, well ahead of factory gate output at 4.0%. Manufacturers are absorbing cost, compressing margins.

markets.name: Euro Area
markets.commentary: Inflation rose to 3.2%, a third straight monthly increase, core up to 2.5%. Energy led at 10.9% and services accelerated to 3.5%. Producer prices at 4.9% sit above consumer inflation, so pipeline pressure has not finished feeding through. May PPI is due early July.

markets.name: United States
markets.commentary: The outlier. Headline reached 4.2% and is still rising monthly, while core held at 2.9%, so the acceleration is almost entirely energy, over 60% of the monthly increase. PPI jumped to 6.5%, the steepest since November 2022, led by energy at 10.7% and wholesale gasoline at 23.4%. Pass through risk is real.

markets.name: Japan
markets.commentary: The calm market. Headline rose to 1.5%, core core (ex fresh food and energy) at 1.8%, the ex fresh food measure at 1.4%. CGPI rose 2.7% with import prices up 3.0% in the month, so cost pressure arrives through imported energy and a weak yen rather than domestic demand.

euroMembers.name: Germany
euroMembers.note: Eased to 2.7% from 2.9% as energy and food cooled, though core firmed to 2.6%. Producer prices a subdued 1.7%.

euroMembers.name: France
euroMembers.note: Rose to 2.8% from 2.5%, but core stayed low at 1.6%, so the move is energy led rather than broad based.

euroMembers.name: Italy
euroMembers.note: Jumped to 3.3% from 2.8%, driven by non-regulated energy prices rising to 12.5%. Core a contained 1.7%.

euroMembers.name: Spain
euroMembers.note: Highest of the four at 3.6%, with core up to 3.0%, the only member of the four showing broad based pressure.

euroMembersNote: Headline HICP year on year is shown on the comparable Eurostat basis (final May 2026). Core measures differ slightly by national office (broadly excluding energy and food). PPI is April 2026, the latest available: Germany +1.7%, Italy +5.1%, euro area aggregate +4.9%. France and Spain country PPI are not in the headline release and are marked n/a.

signals.title: Energy is the shared shock
signals.observe: US wholesale gasoline +23.4%, EA energy +10.9%, UK crude oil inputs +71.8% year on year, Japan import prices +3.0% in the month, with Brent near $105.
signals.why: Energy lands in producer prices first and consumer prices later, so May PPI is a preview of consumer pressure still to come.
signals.action: Lock energy linked contracts and review fuel surcharge clauses now. Stress test budgets for a one to three month consumer pass through, prioritising US and European exposure.

signals.title: Producer prices running ahead of consumer prices everywhere
signals.observe: US PPI 6.5% vs CPI 4.2%, EA 4.9% vs 3.2%, UK input 8.7% vs output 4.0%.
signals.why: Suppliers either absorb the cost and take a margin hit, risking later catch up pricing or distress, or pass it through and lift your input costs directly.
signals.action: Bring forward renegotiation of key supplier contracts. Consider indexation terms that share risk rather than dump it. Watch for price increase requests in Q3.

signals.title: US and Japan inflation gap
signals.observe: US headline 4.2% against Japan 1.5%, a gap with sourcing and currency implications.
signals.why: A strong dollar against a weak yen changes the landed cost maths on US versus Japanese sourced inputs.
signals.action: Revisit sourcing mix toward lower inflation regions where supply is flexible. Hedge material USD exposure where contracts allow.

upcoming: Eurozone PPI for May 2026: early July 2026
UK CPI and PPI for June 2026: mid July 2026
US CPI and PPI for June 2026: mid July 2026
Japan CPI for June 2026: late July 2026
